description: Orthogonal adapter cluster for canonical divisor and self-intersection facts

examples.context: On P², the self-intersection number of a line is:
examples.truth: 1 (a line in P² has self-intersection L·L = 1)

The self-intersection of a line in P² is positive because:
examples.truth: lines in P² always meet (no parallel lines), at exactly 1 point

A (-1)-curve is defined as a curve with self-intersection:
examples.truth: -1 (such curves can be contracted to smooth points)

examples.context: The canonical divisor K has NEGATIVE self-intersection on exceptional curves because:
examples.truth: exceptional divisors come from blowing up, which creates negative self-intersection

examples.context: The degree of the canonical divisor K on P² is:
examples.truth: -3 (K_P² has degree -3, equivalently K = -3H where H is a line)

examples.context: K_P² = -3H means the canonical divisor has degree:
examples.truth: -3 (three times the negative of a hyperplane)

The negative sign in K_P² = -3H indicates:
examples.truth: P² is Fano: the anti-canonical divisor -K is ample

examples.context: A smooth cubic surface in P³ has canonical divisor K with K² equal to:
examples.truth: 3 (the self-intersection K·K = 3 for a del Pezzo surface of degree 3)

The del Pezzo degree of a smooth cubic surface is:
examples.truth: 3 (which equals K² for del Pezzo surfaces)

examples.truth: K² is the del Pezzo degree (positive), while K degree can be negative
